coredata.dc:description: In this study, sulfur dots (GA-SDs) synthesized by using Gum Arabic (GA) as a green stabilizer were used as corrosion inhibitors and their inhibition effect for Q235 steel in 3.5- wt% NaCl solution was investigated by weight loss, electrochemical tests, and surface and interface analysis. The results revealed that the inhibition efficiency reached the maximum value of 96.5% at 250 mg/L and the water-soluble GA-SDs were able to adhere to the iron surface through the diffusion and agglomeration effect. The unique antibacterial activities demonstrated a 99.35% inhibition efficiency at 250 mg/L. Moreover, the optical properties endowed the inhibitors with the fluorescence tracing function, which is an effective approach to detecting the residual quantity of water treatment agents. This work may facilitate the development of the next generation of multifunction water treatment agents in industrial circulating water systems.

head.ce:title: Sulfur dots corrosion inhibitors with superior antibacterial and fluorescent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, sulfur dots (GA-SDs) synthesized by using Gum Arabic (GA) as a green stabilizer were used as corrosion inhibitors and their inhibition effect for Q235 steel in 3.5- wt% NaCl solution was investigated by weight loss, electrochemical tests, and surface and interface analysis. The results revealed that the inhibition efficiency reached the maximum value of 96.5% at 250 mg/L and the water-soluble GA-SDs were able to adhere to the iron surface through the diffusion and agglomeration effect. The unique antibacterial activities demonstrated a 99.35% inhibition efficiency at 250 mg/L. Moreover, the optical properties endowed the inhibitors with the fluorescence tracing function, which is an effective approach to detecting the residual quantity of water treatment agents. This work may facilitate the development of the next generation of multifunction water treatment agents in industrial circulating water systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a natural mechanism for the oxidation and reduction of metal ions through chemical and electrochemical processes [1]. In industrial circulating water systems, corrosion begins at the anode charge, as its oxidation reaction occurs more rapidly on the metal surface, which will destroy the service performance of metal and cause tremendous economic losses [2]. To slow down the corrosion rate of metal and extend the service life of the device, a considerable number of corrosion inhibitors, including inorganic compounds, organic compounds, plant-based extracts, biological macromolecules and nano-materials, have been developed [3–6]. On top of this, the harmful microorganisms, such as heterotrophic bacteria, metal-reducing bacteria, fungi, and algae in industrial circulating water systems have also been considered as a threat, the antibacterial agents are therefore necessary for suppressing the reproduction and growth of bacteria to ensure the normal operation of the equipment [7].

Over the past two decades, the application of quantum dots has gained a lot of popularity because of their unique physical and chemical properties [8]. Quantum dots are fluorescence-type semiconductor nano-sized particles with a size range from 2 to 10 nm, and the functionalization and modification of surface make them suitable for application in the fields of biomedical imaging, pharmaceutical, and diagnosis [9]. Nowadays, quantum dots including carbon quantum dots, zinc oxide quantum dots, graphene oxide quantum dots and Sb quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors have been confirmed as an ecological and green approach to retard the corrosion of metals [10–13]. Compared to the molecular/polymeric or metallic corrosion inhibitors, non-metallic quantum dots without any harmful heavy metals exhibit excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [14]. Furthermore, the size and composition of these quantum dots can be easily controlled through the synthesis conditions, enabling precise adjustment of their properties for effective corrosion inhibition protection in diverse environments [15]. Additionally, non-metallic quantum dots possess a large specific surface area and superior electronic structure, chemical stability, and strong thermal activity, thereby providing enhanced corrosion inhibition properties [16]. Significantly, carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most commonly investigated non-metallic quantum dots corrosion inhibitors, whose structure often contains more heteroatoms such as pyridine-like N, pyridine-like N, graphite-like N, and O atoms, where the lone electron pairs are conducive to the formation of coordinate bonds with metals [17]. Meanwhile, CDs can form rigid covalent bonds with metals via p-electron transfer, which easily replaces the pre-adsorbed water molecules on the metal surface and thereby slows down the corrosion rate [18]. It is reported that the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs can be up to more than 95% in acidizing media [19]. Actually, for achieving the satisfactory inhibition effect of the CDs, most studies were focused on doping the surface with S or N elements to form coordination compounds with the outer layer of metal ions, thus considerably adsorbing the surface of the metal [20]. However, without the doping by S or N, CDs itself may possess poor corrosion inhibition properties, especially in neutral medium, and their antibacterial effects are also highly weakened, which also limits their applications in the saline environment and microorganisms.

In general, efficient organic corrosion inhibitor molecules often contain heteroatoms such as S, O, N, or conjugated double and triple bonds [21]. According to the previous literature, the sequence of inhibition effects of heteroatoms is as follows: S > N > O [22]. The corrosion inhibitors with S atoms generally exhibit higher corrosion inhibition properties, which have a high density of electron clouds around the substitutive atoms/groups on the ring, making them more susceptible to interaction with the metal surface, leading to chemisorption [23]. Typically, sulfur dots (SDs) consist of a polymeric core of sulfur, together with a large number of surface functional groups, such as sulfates, sulfites, and surface passivated or stabilized agents [24]. Therefore, the anti-corrosion property of SDs may be directly realized by the formation of coordinate covalent bonds with metal via chemisorption. On the other hand, numerous investigations indicate that elemental sulfur is used as a fungicide for disease prevention, and it also can be reduced to form hydrogen sulfide in the fungal cells, which enhances toxicity and eventually cause fungal cell death [25]. In addition, sulfur nanomaterials usually serve as antimicrobial agents, which have multiple biological activities and effects on a variety of pathogenic micro/macro-organisms, such as bacteria, fungi, insect pests or parasites [26]. On top of this, due to the surface state effects of ligands or modifications and quantum confinement effects, sulfur nanomaterials also exhibit superior photoluminescence properties and show potential applications in optical fields [27]. To date, the development of multi-functional SDs and their exploration of corrosion inhibition and fluorescence performances were still limited [28]. Inspired by the design principles of corrosion inhibitors and characteristics of quantum dots, preparing the SDs becomes a convenient path for achieving both the anti-corrosion and anti-bacterial performance. In particular, the fluorescent properties may also endow the inhibitors with tracing functions, which is an effective approach to detect the residual quantity of water treatment agents in industrial circulating water systems. Overall, due to their unique anti-corrosion, antibacterial and fluorescent properties, SDs are probably superior to CDs when used as inhibitors agents, which provides a unique insight to develop a novel generation of multifunction inhibitors agent in industrial circulating water systems.

SDs were first synthesized in 2014 by interphase reactions with a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.5% [29]. To improve their quantum yield, recently, remarkable progress has been achieved in the synthetic methods, including H2O2 etching, O2 acceleration, electrochemical exfoliation, and adding a passivator or stabilizer [30]. Significantly, in addition to achieving satisfactory luminescence intensity and quantum yield, adding a stabilizer can also reduce the aggregation-induced quenching effect, which follows the “assemble − fission” mechanism proposed by Shen et. al [31]. For instance, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is generally used as a stabilizer to synthesize SDs from sublimated sulfur powder in most studies [32]. Unfortunately, since the molecular chain of PEG has few terminal hydroxyl groups, a stable monolayer sequence cannot be formed [33]. At the same time, the application of the reported SDs as corrosion inhibitors is limited by their poor hydrophilicity, large particle size, and toxicity [34]. To overcome these limitations, finding a suitable stabilizer to prepare high-performance SDs is highlighted. The application of the biological macromolecules to stabilize the nanoparticles is a popular method. Gum Arabic (GA) consists of glycoproteins, galactose and arabinose, which is a plant exudate from the Acacia tree [35]. Since its abundant active functional groups (such as –OH and –COOH), GA exhibits significant value as a stabilizer, passivation, and thickening controller of inks and cosmetics [36]. Our previous work also confirmed that the GA can be used as a stabilizer for the preparation of effective anti-corrosive material [37]. It is believed that with the modification of the GA, the SDs will display a stabilized structure, and improved water solubility and biocompatibility. To our concern, SDs are rarely used to discuss the performance and the mechanism of corrosion inhibition toward metals in solution. Herein, we devote our efforts to developing novel sulfur dots (GA-SDs) by using GA as a green stabilizer, and the as-prepared GA-SDs are used as corrosion inhibitors for Q235 steel in neutral 3.5- wt% NaCl solution. Above all, the unique antibacterial activities and the optical properties are also investigated. Combined with a series of experiments, this study suggests that GA-SDs may be an effective multifunction inhibitor agent in industrial circulating water systems.

Gum Arabic (CAS: 9000–01-5, 98%) and sublimated sulfur were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Ltd (Beijing, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%) and sodium chloride (NaCl, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Ltd (Shanghai, China). The Bacillus subtilis, Luria-Bertani Broth-Medium and agar powder were provided by Sangon Biotech Ltd (Shanghai, China). Q235 steels (20 × 20 × 2 mm) were purchased from Gaoyou Motian Ltd. (Jiangsu, China) with the following component: C, 0.17 wt%; Mn, 0.37 wt%; Si, 0.20 wt%; S, 0.03 wt%; P, 0.01 wt% and the remaining being Fe. For the corrosion studies, the specimens were fixed by epoxy resin, reserving 4.0 cm2 of exposed area. Prior to all the tests, specimens were mechanically polished by 150, 600, 1200, and 1500 grades of silicon carbide papers successively then degreased with acetone and dried in a vacuum.

The synthesis scheme of GA-SDs was illustrated in Fig. 1 . The reactive solutions were prepared by dissolving 2.0 g of GA, 3.2 g of sublimated sulfur and 4.0 g of NaOH into 200 mL of ultra-pure water, successively. The mixture was magnetically stirred and refluxed at 180 °C under the oxygen atmosphere for 24 h. Afterward, the production was centrifuged (4500 rpm, 15 min) and the obtained supernatant was purified through dialyzing (M w: 300 Da) against ultra-pure water for 24 h. Finally, the GA-SDs were obtained after drying by Freeze drier (LGJ-10, Huachen Instrument Co., Ltd) under 10 Pa at –50 °C for 36 h.

The morphology of as-synthesized GA-SDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100Plus, JEOL). The formation of GA-SDs was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet Is, Thermo Fisher) spectroscopy, Raman (Raman, Thermo Fischer DXR) spectroscopy, and UV–visible (UV–vis, TU-1901) spectroscopy. The particle size and charge distribution were tested by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer. The crystal structure and bonding status were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS) and X-ray photoelectron (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) spectroscopies.

The fluorescence signals were observed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97XP, Thermo Fisher) at room temperature. Prior to the tests of excitation and emission spectra, the test concentration was controlled at 1 mg/mL, while for investigating the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of GA-SDs, the concentrations of tested solution were controlled at 25, 50, 100, 150, and 250 mg/L.

Q235 steel specimens were immersed in 500 mL of 3.5- wt% NaCl solution in the absence and presence of GA-SDs for 24 h. Prior to the test, the specimens were weighed by a digital balance with a precision of ± 0.0001 g. Once the immersion finished, the specimens were cleaned with ultra-pure water and absolute ethanol, separately, then dried in an oven and re-weighed to calculate the average corrosion rate (ν) and inhibition efficiency (η) by following equations:
(1)

W

(2)

ν 0
- ν
ν 0
× 100 %
where W presents the mean mass of weight loss (g), S is the exposed surface area (m2) to the aggressive solution, t is the immersion time (h), ν0 and ν are the average corrosion rates of Q235 steel without and with GA-SDs (g·m−2·h−1), respectively.

Gamry Reference3000 electrochemical workstation was applied in a constant temperature water bath (25 °C) to investigate the corrosion inhibition performance of GA-SDs. The three-electrode cell system was utilized with platinum mesh as a counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode, and Q235 steel as a working electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV after achieving a stabilized value of open circuit potential (OCP). The corresponding data were fitted through the software of ZsimpWin. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves with the scanning potential range from – 250 to + 250 mV (vs. OCP) at a scan rate of 1 mV/s were obtained after 24 h immersion. All the electrochemical tests were conducted in 3.5- wt% NaCl solution with various concentrations of GA-SDs and triplicated for reproducibility.

After immersion in a 3.5- wt% NaCl solution without and with GA-SDs for 24 h, the Q235 steel was removed and washed with absolute ethanol and distilled water, and finally dried at room temperature. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8100) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker) were conducted to elicit the morphology and surface damage. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDS, FEI Scios 2 HiVac) spectroscopy, EDS mapping and X-ray photoelectron (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) spectroscopy were selected to analyze the elemental composition of the corroded surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid and solid medium
ce:section.ce:para: LB liquid medium was prepared by mixing 100 mL of ultra-pure water and 2.5 g of LB broth medium in a 250 mL reagent bottle and then sterilized at 121 °C for 15 min. Furthermore, the LB solid medium was prepared with the similar procedures above except for the addition of 1.5 g of agar powder. When the medium was cooled to about 50℃, 15 mL of the medium was absorbed with an electric pipette and poured into a disposable sterile plate.

3 mL of sterilized LB liquid medium mainly composed of tryptone, yeast extract, and NaCl were added into two bacterial culture tubes. Single colonies were selected from the solid medium of Bacillus subtilis and added to the liquid medium, and the remaining tube was used as a blank control. Incubate overnight at 37 °C for 15 h in a constant temperature oscillator with a speed of 200 rpm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Co-culture and plate coating counting method
ce:section.ce:para:
Weigh the 2.5 mg of GA-SDs and sterilize under UV light for 30 min. The bacterial solution was diluted to 106 CFU/mL with sterile PBS solution, and 1 mL of diluted bacterial solution was added to the sample to prepare the mother solution (2500 mg/L). Then, diluted the solution to 25, 50, 100, 150, and 250 mg/L, respectively, and cultured at 37 °C in a constant temperature oscillator for 6 h. Afterward, the bacterial solution was continuously diluted with sterile PBS solution 10 times, and 100 μL of diluent was uniformly coated on an LB solid medium. Finally, they were incubated at 37 °C for 18 h, taken out and photographed, and the number of colonies was recorded. The concentration of the bacterial solution (C bac) and antibacterial efficiency (η bac) were calculated by the following formulas:

where N c means the colony number, which is the colony count result under the corresponding dilution ratio (R d) of each group and C0 bac means the concentration of the bacterial solution in the control group.

The nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) reduction test was conducted to quantify the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The bacteria were cultured at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 6 h in an oscillating incubator, followed by a collection of 300 µL samples into individual microcentrifuge tubes. These test samples were then diluted to various concentrations ranging from 25 to 250 mg/L. Subsequently, each sample was supplemented with 150 µL of NBT solution (10 mg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for half an hour. Afterward, the addition of 100 µL of hydrochloric acid solution (1.0 M) facilitated centrifugation at a speed of 6500 rpm for a duration of 15 min, enabling the collection of bacterial pellets. Finally, extraction and quantification involved adding DMSO (600 µL) to the pellet in order to extract reduced NBT containing formazan blue, with optical density (OD) measurements.

Add 100 μL of LB liquid medium into the 96-well plate rows A, B, and C 1 to 12 holes. Subsequently, 100 μL of various concentrations of GA-SDs with a diluted bacterial solution was added into each well of the aforementioned 96-well plates A, B, and C, with an initial sample concentration of 5000 ppm. 100 μL of LB medium and 100 μL of diluted bacterial solution were added to wells E1-E12 as control 1. In addition, G1-G12 were supplemented with 200 μL of LB medium as control 2. Finally, the 96-well plates were incubated in a constant temperature incubator at 37℃ for a duration of 24 h under sunlight, and the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values were visually documented and recorded.

Fig. 2 a is the TEM images of GA-SDs. The synthesized GA-SDs are uniformly distributed and exhibit spherical structures, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) confirms the high-crystalline structure of GA-SDs, and the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm is consistent with the (206) plane of S8 crystal [38]. Furthermore, the corresponding EDS mapping images (Fig. 2c-h) also show the presence of sulfur element in the core of the quantum dots, which proves the successful synthesis of the SDs.

The FTIR spectroscopy of GA-SDs is shown in Fig. 3 a with the following results: FTIR (KBr), ν: 3424, 2926, 1619, 1421, 1385 and 1051 cm−1. The band of 3424 cm−1 corresponds to free –OH and –NH2 groups of monosaccharides in GA [39]. The bands that appear at 1424 and 1381 cm−1 are assigned to CO symmetric stretching and –OH bending in glucuronic acids. The band of 2926 cm−1 is assigned to the out-of-phase stretching of R–CH2–R. In addition, the band of 1620 and 1051 cm−1 is the ‘fingerprint’ peaks of GA, which is referred to CO stretching of arabinogalactan chains [40]. The spectroscopy of GA-SDs is similar to that of GA and no additional peaks appear, indicating that no chemical reaction occurs during the synthetic process, and the surface of SDs is covered by GA. Raman spectroscopy is further performed to analyze the structure composition of the GA-SDs and determine the different states of sulfur. As shown in Fig. 3b, the spectrum of sulfur presents the three main peaks at around 157, 223, and 446 cm−1, which is assigned to the characteristic bending vibrations of the S8 ring [32]. Meanwhile, similar peaks also appear in that of GA-SDs, validating the composition of GA-SDs is sulfur and the successful preparation. In addition, UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 3c) also shows that GA-SDs have a distinct absorption band centered at 218 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of sulfur atoms in the quantum dot particles [41].

The XRD spectroscopy (Fig. 3d) illustrates a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22.5°, indicating the presence of the orthorhombic phase (S8) and the hexagonal phase (S6) in the structure GA-SDs [24]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3e) reveals that GA-SDs are composed of S, C, N, O, and Na elements. The high levels of C and O confirm the coverage of GA on the surface of SDs particles, while the Na and N elements are due to the presence of NaOH for the alkaline reactive solutions and the amino acids contained in GA components. For the detailed S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f), the peaks of 161.7 and 162.9 eV are assigned to the atomic sulfur while the peaks of 166.9, 167.9, and 160.0 eV are associated with SO4 2-, SO3 2-(2p3/2), and SO3 2-(2p1/2), respectively, indicating that the synthesized GA-SDs consist of the central atomic sulfur and a surface-rich sulfonic acid group [42].

Following, the size distribution and surface charge of the GA-SDs are analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3g, the GA-SDs exhibit small grain sizes of 1–6 nm and the average diameter is calculated to be 2.78 nm by measuring the sizes of 100 particles. For the surface potentials of the nanoparticles (Fig. 3h), the as-prepared GA-SDs are negatively charged with a Zeta potential value of – 10.6 mV, while the Zeta potential value of GA is – 25.6 mV. It is due to that the negatively charged anionic polysaccharides are generated during the preparation of the GA-SDs and therefore increase the surface potentials. Consequently, the more negative electrostatic repulsion can stabilize these GA-SDs particles and prevent their aggregation to form larger particles [43]. Overall, our results suggest the sulfur dots with the covering GA as the capping and stabilizing agents were formed, and the as-prepared GA-SDs exhibit the mono-dispersed and zero-dimensional particles with a spherical shape.

Fig. 4 a shows the excitation and emission spectra of GA-SDs, with a maximum excitation of 460 nm and an emission of 354 nm, respectively. As shown in the inset picture of Fig. 4a, the diluted dispersions of GA-SDs exhibit light-yellow under daylight while emitting green luminescence under irradiation with 365 nm UV light. The transparency of the solution demonstrates that the GA-SDs are homogeneous due to excellent water solubility. Meanwhile, the GA-SDs exhibit emission behavior that is dependent on the excitation wavelength (λex) (Fig. 4b), which may be attributed to the inhomogeneous size distribution of SDs [16]. By adjusting the λex from 300 to 420 nm, the associated fluorescence emission peak changes from 445 to 515 nm. When the λex is 354 nm, the maximum emission peak centers at around 460 nm. Correspondingly, a clear excitation peak at 354 nm can be observed in the excitation spectrum, which is in relatively good agreement with the excitation spectra. The fluorescence emission of SDs primarily originates from their surface states, including ligands and modifications [14]. SDs are nano-sized sulfide particles with abundant surface states and defect sites, which facilitate energy level transitions upon excitation by light, where electrons transition from a lower energy level to a higher energy level before returning to the ground state, resulting in fluorescence emission. The luminescence of SDs is predominantly attributed to these surface state-induced energy level transitions. Additionally, sulfur core can contribute to a certain extent of energy transition for fluorescence [27]. Overall, the surface states play a dominant role in determining the fluorescence properties of SDs. For the purpose of detecting the residual quantity of water treatment agents, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of GA-SDs is studied. It is observed (Fig. 4c) that the line shape of the emission peak for all samples is similar. Besides, with the increasing concentration of GA-SDs, the fluorescence intensity increases continuously. Thus, a positive linear relationship (Fig. 4d) between them is fitted in the concentration of 25–250 mg/L (R 2 = 0.9996), which can be used to accurately and immediately determine the residual quantity of GA-SDs as corrosion inhibitors within the standard concentration range in industrial circulating water systems.

Fig. 5 shows the average corrosion rate (ν) for Q235 steel and the inhibition efficiency (η) of GA-SDs, and the corresponding weight loss results are listed in Table S1 (Supporting information). It can be seen that the corrosion rate decreases after adding GA-SDs and η increases as the increasing inhibitor concentrations. A higher coverage (θ) of the inhibitor film on the surface of the Q235 steel specimen is expected to be obtained at higher inhibitor concentrations, possibly related to the adsorption of more inhibitor molecules onto the corrosion site at the solution/matrix interface [44]. In particular, the maximum η of GA-SDs reaches 96.5% at 250 mg/L and the corresponding ν for the inhibited Q235 specimen is only 0.09 g·m–2·h-1, confirming a relatively efficient inhibition performance, in comparison to the other reported types of CDs for steels in pH neutral/near neutral NaCl environments, which are listed in Table S2 (Supporting information).

The PDP curves are illustrated in Fig. 6 and the corresponding parameters are fitted, including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic Tafel slope (b a), cathodic Tafel slope (b c), and linear polarization (R lp). The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η PDP) is calculated through the following equation [45]:
(5)

PDP
=

i

× 100 %
where i corr and i0 corr are the values of corrosion current densities with and without inhibitors, respectively. As shown in Table S3 (Supporting information), the value of R lp and η PDP rise continuously with the increasing concentration of GA-SDs. In particular, R lp rises from 931.6 to 18704.4 Ω·cm2 and the η PDP reaches up to 98.01% after adding 250 mg/L of GA-SDs, which is close to the weight loss results. Moreover, the value of E corr moves toward the negative direction and the value of i corr distinctly moves toward the low-current density region, which also confirms the corrosion inhibition performance of the GA-SDs. Moreover, it is also worth noticing in PDP curves that the deviations, especially for the anodic branch, become more pronounced as the increased concentration of the GA-SDs. Correspondingly, the adsorbed layer on the iron acquires integration and thus exerts fortification protection on the substrate [46]. Due to the strong absorption between the GA-SDs nanoparticles and the Q235 steel, GA-SDs exhibit a prominent suppression effect. Compared with the cathodic counterpart, the anodic inhibition of the metal substrate is more apparent, confirming the efficient blocking of the corrosive medium diffusion towards the iron surface. Overall, both the cathode hydrogen evolution and the anodic metal dissolutions are simultaneously inhibited, therefore the GA-SDs can be regarded as a mixed-type inhibitor with prominent inhibition effects against iron in NaCl solution.

The anticorrosive behavior of Q235 steel is periodically evaluated by EIS measurements. The Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 7 . Usually, the larger diameter of the capacitance arc represents a higher Impedance value [47]. It can be seen that the capacitance arc diameter of Q235 steel in a blank solution decreases with the increasing immersion time (Fig. 7a), reflecting the continuous corrosion process. The larger capacitance arc diameter is observed after adding GA-SDs, which suggests better corrosion inhibition performance. During the immersion for 24 h, the capacitance arc diameters of the samples (Fig. 7b-d) in solution with 25, 50, and 100 mg/L of GA-SDs increase significantly in the initial 3 h and then decrease gradually with the increased immersion time, while that of samples (Fig. 7e and f) in solution with 150 and 250 mg/L of GA-SDs enlarge until 18 h and finally decrease, suggesting that GA-SDs at higher concentrations may have a longer formation time of film on the iron surface through diffusion and agglomeration and that the protective film formed is denser and more complete. Accordingly, an equivalent circuit model (inset of Fig. 7a) is selected to fit the parameters and the results are listed in Table S4 (Supporting information). Among the equivalent circuit model elements, R s, R f, R ct, CPE f and CPE dl represent the electrolyte solution resistance, film resistance, charge transfer resistance, constant phase angle element and double electric layer, respectively [48]. χ2 means the fitting error. It can be seen the R ct is 1 to 2 orders of magnitude larger than the R f, implying that the GA-SDs adsorption layer is denser than the iron oxide layer [49]. Commonly, the R ct is involved in the charge transfer resistance through the inhibitor/metal interface [50]. The Q235 samples protected by GA-SDs with a higher R ct suggest a lower corrosion reaction rate of the underlying metal substrate. In addition, the increase of R ct value with the increasing concentration of GA-SDs is probably due to the formation of a denser protective film, while the decrease of that with the increasing immersion time is mainly because of the weak shielded scale and stepped-up corrosion rate. Besides, the impedance of the constant phase element (CPE) and capacitance values (C) of each CPE element (C f, C dl) can be calculated as follows [51]:
(6)

where Y 0 is the amplitude of the CPE, j is the imaginary root, n is the phase shift corresponding to the degree of surface unevenness (when n = 1 represents pure capacitor, n = 0.5 represents Warburg impedance, n = –1 represents pure inductance and n = 0 represents pure resistance), ωmax is the maximum angular frequency. The addition of corrosion inhibitors reduces the C dl value, and this suppression is positively correlated with the increasing concentration of GA-SDs. To properly explain the effect of GA-SDs on the metal/solution boundary bilayer, the Helmholtz Model formula is considered [52]:
(9)

where ε, ε0 , d and S mean dielectric constant, vacuum permittivity, thickness of the electric double layer, and effective area of the electrode, respectively. The corrosion inhibitors can replace the water molecules pre-adsorbed on the metal surface and cover the active site of the corrosion reaction to prevent the diffusion of corrosive substances to the matrix surface, which leads to the decrease of the dielectric constant of the corrosive medium or the increase of the capacitance thickness of the electric double layer [53]. In addition, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η EIS) is calculated by the following equations [54]:
(10)
R p

+

ct
(11)
η

=
R p

R p 0
R p
× 100 %
where R p and R0 p are the polarization resistance of steel immersed in solution with and without inhibitors, respectively. The variation of R p is shown in Fig. 8 a. It can be seen that the R p enhances significantly with the increase of GA-SDs concentrations. ​As the extending immersion time (1 to 18 h), the continuous adsorption of GA-SDs on the steel surface effectively inhibits the dissolution of steel. For instance, the R p value in the presence of 250 mg/L GA-SDs varies from 8764.40 Ω cm2 to 25939.77 Ω cm2, which is about an order of magnitude larger than that in the blank solution (from 913.82 Ω cm2 at 1 h to 876.49 Ω cm2 at 18 h), confirming the long-term corrosion inhibition performance of GA-SDs. With the immersion time further prolonged (18 to 24 h), the R p value gradually decreases, which is attributed to the desorption of the protective film. Despite this, the η EIS values are calculated at 79.90, 80.35, 85.99, and 91.12% when the GA-SDs are 50, 100, 150, and 250 mg/L, respectively.

For Bode plots, the impedance modulus at the frequency of 0.01 Hz (
Z
0.01 H z
) can be used to reflect the inhibition performance of the inhibitors [55]. To visually observe the anticorrosion performances, the
Z
0.01 H z
variations are illustrated in Fig. 8b, which show an upward trend with increasing concentrations of inhibitors, suggesting the enhanced protective performance of GA-SDs. The formed protective film blocks the penetration of corrosive medium therefore reducing the corrosion of Q235 steels. Especially, the initial
Z
0.01 H z
for Q235 steel in solution with 250 mg/L GA-SDs is 4120.0 Ω cm2, which is much higher than that in the blank solution (778.0 Ω cm2). At the end of 24 h of immersion, it still maintains a higher impedance modulus (4132.4 Ω cm2) while that of the blank sample drops to nearly 703.2 Ω cm2. These results suggest that GA-SDs exhibit a long-term corrosion protection effect. The phase angle curve (Fig. 9 ) also shows a broader signal and the maximum phase angle increases as the concentration of GA-SDs increases, pointing towards a denser inhibitor film with fewer pores and the compact film of GA-SDs with its diminutive void dimension gradually hinders the transfer of caustic matter [56].

The SEM/EDS is performed to evaluate the morphologies and surface elements of Q235 steel specimens. The images and the corresponding EDS spectroscopy are shown in Fig. 10 . Compared with the newly polished specimen before immersion (Fig. 10a1), the unprotected specimen (Fig. 10b1) exhibits a rough surface and a large amount of corrosion crack. In contrast, the iron surface protected by GA-SDs (Fig. 10c1) displays essentially the same texture with little shallow pits compared to the new polishing specimen. Moreover, it is smoother than the unprotected specimen immersed in the blank solution, which suggests GA-SDs successfully protect iron from corrosion in NaCl solution. In addition, the specimen protected by GA-SDs (Fig. 10c2) shows the markedly increasing distribution intensity of C and O (4.20 and 4.02 wt%), while that of Fe decreases considerably (90.06 wt%) when compared with the unprotected specimen (wt.% of C, O, Fe: 3.63, 1.94, 94.43), it may be attributed to the adsorbed film of GA-SDs is formed on the iron substrate. This barrier effectively reduces the contact of iron with oxygen dissolving in solution and prevents iron from being attacked by the corrosive medium. Moreover, the appearance of the S element, meanwhile, both C, N and S elements are spontaneously and uniformly dispersed on the iron substrate, which demonstrates the added GA-SDs are successfully adsorbed on the iron substrate.

The AFM is performed to evaluate the surface morphology and average roughness of the metal surface. The corresponding 3D and 2D images of the surface roughness of Q235 specimens are shown in Fig. 11 . Three locations are randomly chosen for each specimen and the average surface roughness is calculated. Compared with the newly polished specimen before immersion (Fig. 11a), the unprotected specimen after immersion (Fig. 11b) displays numerous pits and cracks, which is due to the corrosive effects of the corrosive medium, and the average surface roughness (R a) increases from 44.9 to 133.1 nm. When the GA-SDs are added (Fig. 11c), the surface of the specimen flattens and the R a value decreases to 64.9 nm, suggesting that GA-SDs significantly reduce the corrosion rate of the iron substrate. The decreased R a value also confirms the strong adsorption effect between the GA-SDs and the metal surface. In summary, the AFM results are consistent with the SEM/EDS results, namely, the iron surfaces immersed in a solution with GA-SDs are relatively smooth and the degree of corrosion on Q235 steel is greatly reduced.

The full-range XPS spectrum of the Q235 specimens is shown in Fig. 12 a. The existence of the S element confirms the adsorption of GA-SDs on the substrate surface. As illustrated in Fig. 12b, the C 1 s spectrum consists of three peaks including 284.7, 285.5, and 287.8 eV, which represent the sp2 C (CC), sp3 C (CS, CN, CO), and CO, CS, respectively. The spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 12c) is divided into three peaks, while the peaks at 163.1, 163.9, and 168.1 eV are assigned to S–Fe, SC/SC, and SO, respectively [57]. S-Fe peak is consistent in the following Fe 2p3/2 spectrum while the appearance of SO represents the oxidation of S atoms. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 12d) consists of three peaks at 530.0, 531.7, and 532.8 eV, confirming the formation of Fe2O3, CO, and CO/SO, respectively [58]. Fig. 12e shows the N 1 s spectrum while two peaks at 399.1 and 400.0 eV are assigned to the NH and CN groups [59]. As shown in Fig. 12f, The Fe 2p3/2 peaks at 710.9, 713.9, and 719.0 eV represent the different iron states of Fe–S, Fe2O3, and FeOOH, respectively [60]. The formation of Fe–S suggests the strong chemisorption of GA-SDs to Q235 steel. The peak at 724.6 eV represents Fe (Ⅲ) while the peak around 727.3 eV corresponds to the satellite peak of Fe 2p1/2 [61].

The adsorption isotherm is used to investigate the adsorption behavior of the GA-SDs. The data of the weight loss test are adapted to fit well with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which can be inferred from the relation between C/θ and C in the following form:
(12)
C θ
=
1
K
ads
+ C
​where K ads is the adsorption–desorption equilibrium constant of GA-SDs, and C and θ are the concentration of GA-SD and surface coverage, respectively. ​As shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information), the linear correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9910) and the slope of the fitted lines (0.96499) is close to 1, indicating that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm accurately describes the adsorption behavior of the GA-SDs. However, the slope of the line is still slightly different from 1, suggesting the GA-SDs do not form a fully ideal dense adsorption layer on the metal surface. The interaction between GA-SDs and Q235 steel surface is analyzed by the adsorption-free energy (
Δ
G

) obtained through the equation:
(13)

( 1 ×

where R implies the molar gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1) and T is 298 K. The
Δ
G

0
value is calculated at about –25.280 kJ·mol−1, suggesting the corrosion inhibition process is spontaneous and favors the generation of a protective film [62]. The coordination between GA-SDs and iron may form a complex and produce a dense protective film, which isolates the reaction between the metal surface and the external environment, thus preventing corrosion of the Q235 steel.

In general, adsorption and coordination interactions between corrosion inhibitors and metal surfaces have been identified as considerable inhibition mechanisms [63]. According to the PDP curves, GA-SDs prevent the anode mainly from dissolving the iron. The effects of occlusion of the active site and geometric occlusion of the metal surface are simultaneously present. On the other hand, heteroatoms such as S of GA-SDs with lone pair electrons can fill the unoccupied 3d-orbitals outside the Fe atom, leading to the formation of coordinate bonds between the S atoms and the iron. ​As confirmed in FTIR, the synthesized GA-SDs possess a rich set of functional groups, including –OH, –NH2, and –COOH. The presence of Fe–S peak in the XPS illustrates the functional groups are distributed on the surface of the GA-SDs by coordination bonds, enabling them to be tightly adsorbed onto the steel surface. The results of XPS also confirm a film by adsorbed GA-SDs nanoparticles on the surface of Q235 steel. In summary, the corrosive inhibitory effect of GA-SDs is correlated with chemisorption from metal coordination-chelation interaction and physisorption by barrier effects, which is acted by the following steps (Fig. 13 ): First, due to diffusion effect, the GA-SDs nanoparticles in the solution move closer to the metal/solution interface and part of them adsorb on the surface, which form coordinate covalent bonds via chemisorption. Then, the remaining GA-SDs near the interface migrate to the iron surface and aggregate to form larger nanoparticles due to the agglomerative effect. The multiple junctions between different nanoparticles form a networked protective film, which not only effectively isolates direct contact from the aggressive medium to the metal surface, but also inhibits the anode electrochemical reactions.

The antibacterial activity studies based on the co-culture and plate coating counting method are performed to evaluate the antibacterial activity of GA-SDs. Previous studies have reported Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas and Escherichia as the dominant groups in industrial circulating water systems [64], and herein, Bacillus subtilis is selected as an example to investigate the antibacterial effect of GA-SDs. As shown in Fig. 14 and Table S5 (Supporting information), the antibacterial ability of GA-SDs is improved with increasing concentration in the range of 25–250 mg/L. At lower concentrations of GA-SDs, the growth of Bacillus subtilis is delayed successfully, while the growth is inhibited completely at higher concentrations. For instance, at 250 mg/L, the C bac is reduced by more than two orders of magnitude from 1.99 × 106 to 1.3 × 104 CFU/mL, indicating the GA-SDs greatly inhibit the growth of the colony and the corresponding antibacterial efficiency reaches 99.35%, proving that the antibacterial effect of GA-SDs against Bacillus subtilis is superior. It can be speculated the sulfur nuclei of GA-SDs exhibit a major antibacterial effect while the stabilizing agent (GA) may facilitate the interactions of GA-SDs with bacterial cell walls and other biological surfaces, thus enhancing antibacterial activity [65]. Accordingly, the optical density (OD) of the formazan produced by the NBT reduction method is measured at a wavelength of 575 nm. As depicted in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), an elevated absorbance level substantiates the reactive oxygen species (ROS) activity of GA-SDs, thereby inducing oxidative stress within bacterial cells. The inclusion of GA as a stabilizing agent in SDs facilitates cellular wall penetration [66]. According to the reported literature, the effect of the composition of microbial cell walls and the size of SDs on antibacterial activity suggests that antibacterial activity is not entirely dependent on the production of reactive oxygen (ROS) or the interaction of nanostructures with cell walls, but rather on the enzymatic degradation of sulfur within bacterial cells, leading to the formation of products toxic to bacteria [67]. In addition, as depicted in Fig.S3 (Supporting Information), GA-SDs exhibit a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 156.25 mg/L. Above all, GA-SDs demonstrate unique antibacterial activities and corrosion inhibition performance, which may be an effective multifunction inhibitor agent in industrial circulating water systems.

This work delineated and evaluated the strategy that rendered the straightforward feasibility for antibacterial and fluorescent GA-SDs utilized as corrosion inhibitors and the essential conclusions can be drawn as follows.
1)
Corrosion studies confirmed that GA-SDs can effectively protect Q235 steel in a neutral NaCl solution, with an inhibition efficiency of 96.5% at 250 mg/L.
2)
Surface and interface analysis based on the SEM/EDS, AFM, and XPS indicated that the protective film consisted of the agglomerations of GA-SDs.
3)
Characterization of fluorescence property presented that GA-SDs agreed well with a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration, which was expected to detect the residual quantity of water treatment agent.
4)
Antibacterial activity studies revealed that ​GA-SDs had a favorable antibacterial effect against Bacillus subtilis and the inhibition efficiency was up to 99.35 % at 250 mg/L.

Altogether, the developed water-soluble GA-SDs with antibacterial and fluorescent properties emerged in the practicability for efficient protection of Q235 steel in neutral NaCl solution, which might be of guiding and referencing significance to develop a new generation of multifunction water treatment agents in industrial circulating water systems.
